name: inception

members.name: InceptionConfig
members.signature: InceptionConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, version: str = 'v3', aux_logits: bool = False, dropout: float = 0.5)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, version: str = 'v3', aux_logits: bool = False, dropout: float = 0.5)

members.extended:
`aux_logits` enables the auxiliary classifier that attaches after the
second InceptionC block (used during training with weight 0.4).
`dropout` — main head dropout rate (0.5 in the paper).
`version` — only `"v3"` is supported in this module.

members.name: InceptionV3
members.extended:
Implements the topology from Szegedy et al., "Rethinking the
Inception Architecture for Computer Vision", CVPR 2016: a deep
pre-Inception stem (3×Conv → MaxPool → 2×Conv → MaxPool), three
`_InceptionA` blocks at $35\times35$, a Reduction-A
block (`_InceptionB`) down to $17\times17$, four
`_InceptionC` blocks with factorised $1\times7$ and
$7\times1$ convolutions, a Reduction-B block
(`_InceptionD`) down to $8\times8$, two
`_InceptionE` blocks with the expanded
$1\times3$/$3\times1$ parallel branches, and a final
`lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d` to $1\times1$.  Designed
for $299\times299$ RGB inputs.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `inception_v3` for the
paper-cited configuration.  `aux_logits` only affects the
classifier variant.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception import inception_v3
>>> backbone = inception_v3()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 2048, 1, 1)
(1, 2048, 1, 1)
notes:
The headline factorisation idea is to replace an
$n\times n$ convolution with an $n\times1$ followed by
a $1\times n$ convolution: for $n = 7$ this trades
$49 C^2$ parameters for $14 C^2$ while adding an extra
ReLU and matching the original receptive field along a separable
manifold.  Inception v3 uses three block topologies (A, B, C) at
three spatial resolutions (35×35, 17×17, 8×8), each tailored to
the receptive-field budget at that stage.  Approximately 23.8 M
parameters (without auxiliary head); achieves a top-5 ImageNet
error of 3.5% on the validation set.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: inception_a0, inception_a1, inception_a2
attributes.description:
Three Inception-A blocks at $35\times35$ (factorised
$5\times5$ into two $3\times3$).

attributes.name: inception_c0, inception_c1, inception_c2, inception_c3
attributes.description:
Four Inception-C blocks at $17\times17$ with
$1\times7$ / $7\times1$ factorisation.

attributes.name: inception_e0, inception_e1
attributes.description:
Two Inception-E blocks at $8\times8$ with expanded
$1\times3$ / $3\times1$ parallel branches.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed via
`BackboneMixin`.

members.name: InceptionV3ForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Inception v3 image classifier with optional auxiliary classifier.
members.extended:
Combines an `InceptionV3` backbone with a global-average-pool
+ `lucid.nn.Dropout` (`p=config.dropout`, default 0.5) +
`lucid.nn.Linear` head producing `config.num_classes`
logits.  When `config.aux_logits=True`, an auxiliary classifier
(`_InceptionAux`) attaches after the last
$17\times17$ Inception-C block and contributes a 0.4-weighted
cross-entropy term during training.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use `inception_v3_cls` for the
timm-compatible default (no auxiliary head).  Pass
`aux_logits=True` to enable the auxiliary classifier — used
during training to combat vanishing gradients and provide
intermediate supervision.

examples:
Inference path (auxiliary head ignored even if enabled):
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception import inception_v3_cls
>>> model = inception_v3_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
notes:
From Szegedy et al., "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for
Computer Vision", CVPR 2016, §6.  Total loss with auxiliary head is

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}
    + 0.4 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}.
$$
The paper also introduces *label smoothing* — replacing the
one-hot target with $(1 - \epsilon)\,\mathbf{1}_y + \epsilon/K$ — although that is a training-loop concern handled
outside this module.  Final top-5 ImageNet validation error in the
paper is 3.5% with ≈24 M parameters.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: drop
attributes.description:
Dropout applied before the main classifier
(`p=config.dropout`, 0.5 in the paper).

members.name: InceptionV3Output
members.signature: InceptionV3Output(logits: Tensor, aux_logits: Tensor | None = None, loss: Tensor | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(logits: Tensor, aux_logits: Tensor | None = None, loss: Tensor | None = None)

members.extended:
Carries the main classifier logits and the optional auxiliary
classifier logits emitted during training only.  The auxiliary head
in Inception v3 attaches after the last 17x17 stage
(`inception_c3` / Mixed_6e in the paper diagram) and is only
active when both `config.aux_logits=True` and the model is in
training mode.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description: Main classifier output of shape `(B, num_classes)`.

parameters.name: aux_logits
parameters.description:
Logits from the auxiliary classifier of shape
`(B, num_classes)`; `None` at inference or when
`aux_logits=False`.

parameters.name: loss
parameters.description:
Cross-entropy loss with auxiliary term (weight 0.4) when labels
were passed to `forward`; `None` otherwise.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception import inception_v3_cls
>>> model = inception_v3_cls(aux_logits=True).eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.aux_logits is None   # inactive at eval
True
notes:
From Szegedy et al., "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for
Computer Vision", CVPR 2016, §6.  Training loss with the auxiliary
head is

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}
    + 0.4 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}.
$$

members.name: inception_v3
members.extended:
Builds an `InceptionV3` with the paper-cited Szegedy 2016
topology: deep stem → 3× Inception-A → Reduction-A → 4× Inception-C
→ Reduction-B → 2× Inception-E → $1\times1$ global pool.
Designed for $299\times299$ RGB inputs.  Approximately
21.8 M parameters in the backbone alone.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `InceptionConfig`.
Note that `aux_logits` only affects the classifier variant.

returns.description:
Backbone with the Inception v3 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception import inception_v3
>>> model = inception_v3()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 2048, 1, 1)
(1, 2048, 1, 1)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for
Computer Vision", CVPR 2016.  Top-5 ImageNet validation error in
the paper is 3.5% (full classifier).  Single architecture; no
paper-cited size variants (H11).

members.name: inception_v3_cls
members.summary: Inception v3 image classifier (no auxiliary head by default).
members.extended:
Builds an `InceptionV3ForImageClassification` with the
paper-cited Szegedy 2016 topology.  Default configuration matches
the standard reference-framework setting of `aux_logits=False` —
approximately 23.8 M parameters; enable the auxiliary classifier
by passing `aux_logits=True` (adds ≈4 M parameters used only
during training).  Designed for $299\times299$ RGB inputs;
achieves a top-5 ImageNet validation error of 3.5%.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `InceptionConfig`.
Common picks: `num_classes=N` to retarget the head,
`aux_logits=True` to enable the auxiliary classifier,
`dropout=p` to adjust regularisation strength.

returns.description:
Classifier with the Inception v3 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.inception import inception_v3_cls
>>> model = inception_v3_cls().eval()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 299, 299)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 1000)
notes:
See Szegedy et al., "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for
Computer Vision", CVPR 2016, §6.  Training with the auxiliary head
adds a 0.4-weighted cross-entropy term as

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}
    + 0.4 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}.
$$

summary: Inception v3 family — Szegedy et al., 2015.
citation: Szegedy, Christian, et al. "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for Computer Vision." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2016, pp. 2818–2826.
theory:
Inception v3 is the third generation of the Inception family and
crystallises a set of *design principles* that the authors
extracted from earlier experiments — most notably that one should
avoid representational bottlenecks (do not aggressively shrink
spatial resolution in early layers) and should *factorise* large
convolutions to gain both parameter efficiency and additional
nonlinearity.

The headline factorisation idea is to replace a single
$n\times n$ convolution with an $n\times1$ followed by
a $1\times n$ convolution.  For $n=7$ this trades
$49 C^2$ parameters for $14 C^2$ while adding an
intermediate ReLU and matching the original receptive field
exactly along a separable manifold.  Inception v3 uses three
different block topologies (Inception-A, B, C) at three spatial
resolutions (35×35, 17×17, 8×8), each tailored to the receptive-
field budget at that stage of the network.

Two further innovations debuted in this paper are *label
smoothing*, which replaces the one-hot target with a softer
distribution $(1 - \epsilon)\,\mathbf{1}_y +
\epsilon/K$ to discourage overconfident predictions, and
*RMSProp* with a carefully tuned learning-rate schedule for
ImageNet-scale training.  Inception v3 reaches a top-5 error of
3.5% on ImageNet validation with only 24 M parameters,
illustrating that thoughtful architectural surgery can outperform
brute scaling.
